description: Establish a structured testing and code review process before deployment to catch defects early, reducing the likelihood of technical issues escalating.
implementation_steps: Unit test critical modules before integration
Conduct peer code review for all major changes
Maintain a staging environment separate from production
Log and triage bugs in a shared issue tracker

description: Reduce dependence on an unreliable power supply for IT infrastructure through backup power and cloud-based hosting where feasible.
implementation_steps: Install UPS units for critical servers and POS terminals
Host the core system with a cloud provider offering redundant power
Schedule automated data backups during stable power windows
Document a manual fallback procedure for outages

description: Reduce integration risk with third-party APIs (e.g. mobile money providers) through sandbox testing and a phased rollout.
implementation_steps: Test the integration thoroughly in the provider's sandbox environment before going live
Implement error handling and retry logic for all external API calls
Run a pilot with a small user group before full rollout
Maintain a manual fallback transaction process during the pilot

description: Protect against data loss during system migration through backup and validation procedures.
implementation_steps: Take a full backup of source data before migration begins
Run the migration on a copy or staging database first
Validate record counts and key fields after migration
Keep the old system accessible in read-only mode during transition

description: Manage integration challenges with legacy systems through incremental interfacing rather than a full rip-and-replace approach.
implementation_steps: Map data flows between the legacy system and the new system before development
Build a translation/interface layer rather than modifying the legacy system directly
Run both systems in parallel during a transition period
Retire legacy components only after the new system is fully validated

description: Ensure acceptable application performance on the low-end Android devices common among SME staff and customers in Zambia.
implementation_steps: Test the application on low-cost, low-memory devices, not just flagship phones
Optimise images and minimise data payloads for low-bandwidth conditions
Provide a lightweight or offline-first mode where feasible
Monitor crash and performance analytics after release

description: Address insufficient testing environments by provisioning a dedicated staging setup separate from production.
implementation_steps: Provision a low-cost staging environment using free-tier cloud resources
Mirror production configuration as closely as possible in staging
Require all changes to pass staging tests before production deployment
Document known differences between staging and production

description: Reduce vendor lock-in risk from proprietary systems that are costly or difficult to replace.
implementation_steps: Favour open standards and exportable data formats where possible
Negotiate data portability and exit clauses into vendor contracts
Maintain regular exports of critical data in non-proprietary formats
Periodically review alternative vendors to preserve negotiating leverage

description: Reduce risk from inadequate technical documentation that slows onboarding and troubleshooting.
implementation_steps: Maintain a living technical README covering setup, configuration and common issues
Document architecture decisions and rationale as the system evolves
Require documentation updates as part of the definition of done for major changes
Store documentation centrally and accessibly, not on individual machines

description: Plan server and infrastructure capacity ahead of anticipated business growth to avoid performance degradation.
implementation_steps: Forecast expected user and transaction growth over 12-24 months
Choose a hosting plan or provider that allows straightforward scaling
Load-test the system under simulated peak demand before major campaigns
Monitor resource utilisation and set upgrade triggers in advance

description: Build buffer time into the schedule for activities dependent on external suppliers or contractors.
implementation_steps: Add contingency time to milestones involving imports or third parties
Identify critical path activities and monitor them weekly
Agree delivery timelines and penalties in supplier contracts where possible
Maintain a shortlist of alternative local suppliers

description: Manage scope creep that threatens the schedule through formal change control.
implementation_steps: Require written sign-off for any scope change
Assess schedule and cost impact of each change request before approval
Hold a weekly review of pending change requests
Communicate the impact of approved changes to all stakeholders

description: Plan around seasonal disruptions, such as the rainy season, affecting logistics and site access.
implementation_steps: Schedule equipment deliveries and site work outside peak rainy months where possible
Identify alternative transport routes or providers in advance
Build extra lead time into procurement during October to April
Communicate likely seasonal delays to stakeholders ahead of time

description: Reduce schedule dependency on a single external consultant or expert for critical path tasks.
implementation_steps: Identify and brief a backup resource for key roles
Document processes and decisions as the project progresses
Schedule consultant work in defined blocks with clear deliverables
Build in a short overlap period for handover to internal staff

description: Reduce the impact of underestimated task durations through structured estimation techniques.
implementation_steps: Use three-point estimation (optimistic, likely, pessimistic) for key tasks
Compare estimates against actuals from previous similar projects
Review and adjust estimates at each milestone rather than only at project start
Involve the people who will do the work in estimating it

description: Reduce schedule risk from dependency on a single supplier for a critical-path deliverable.
implementation_steps: Identify all single-supplier dependencies on the critical path early
Qualify at least one backup supplier for critical items
Order critical items with maximum lead time ahead of need
Track supplier performance against agreed delivery dates

description: Account for public holidays and festive-season slowdowns that commonly affect Zambian business operations in December and around Easter.
implementation_steps: Mark national holidays and typical festive slowdown periods on the project calendar
Avoid scheduling critical milestones during these periods where possible
Confirm supplier and contractor availability ahead of the festive season
Build extra buffer into any activity spanning December-January

description: Reduce delays caused by slow stakeholder decision-making at key milestones.
implementation_steps: Set clear decision deadlines when requesting stakeholder input
Escalate to a designated decision-maker if deadlines are missed
Present options with a recommended default to speed up decisions
Document decisions and timestamps to maintain accountability

description: Avoid underestimating the time required for user-acceptance testing (UAT).
implementation_steps: Allocate a dedicated UAT phase in the schedule rather than treating it as a buffer
Recruit UAT participants and confirm their availability well in advance
Prepare test scripts and scenarios before UAT begins
Plan at least one round of fixes and re-testing after initial UAT feedback

description: Manage schedule risk arising from resource contention when staff are shared across multiple projects.
implementation_steps: Maintain a shared resource calendar visible to all project leads
Negotiate protected time blocks for staff working on critical tasks
Flag and resolve resourcing conflicts at the earliest planning stage
Escalate persistent contention to management for prioritisation

description: Manage currency risk on imported software and hardware by budgeting in foreign currency with a buffer.
implementation_steps: Add a 10-15% contingency to budgets for items priced in foreign currency
Negotiate fixed ZMW prices with local suppliers where possible
Monitor exchange rates and time purchases when rates are favourable
Separate local-currency and foreign-currency lines in the budget

description: Control consultant and contractor cost overruns through fixed-price or capped contracts.
implementation_steps: Agree fixed-price contracts for well-defined deliverables
Require detailed quotes before work begins
Track actual versus budgeted spend on a weekly basis
Review and approve any additional work requests before they proceed

description: Avoid underestimating recurring costs such as cloud hosting and software subscriptions.
implementation_steps: Obtain at least two quotes and pricing tiers from cloud providers before committing
Model 12-month cost projections including expected usage growth
Set usage alerts and budget caps with the provider where available
Review recurring costs on a quarterly basis

description: Protect the project contingency reserve from premature depletion.
implementation_steps: Require management approval to draw from the contingency reserve
Track contingency drawdown against overall project progress
Re-baseline the budget if contingency falls below a defined threshold
Report contingency status at every project review

description: Reduce the impact of inaccurate initial cost estimates through structured, evidence-based budgeting.
implementation_steps: Base estimates on actual costs from comparable past projects where available
Break the budget down into detailed line items rather than lump sums
Have a second person independently review major cost estimates
Revisit and refine estimates as the project scope firms up

description: Account for hidden fees charged by international payment processors and card networks on cross-border transactions.
implementation_steps: Review the full fee schedule, not just the headline transaction rate, before selecting a processor
Compare at least two processors for cross-border and currency conversion fees
Build processor fees explicitly into the project's financial model
Periodically audit statements against the agreed fee schedule

description: Manage cash-flow risk arising from delays in loan or financing disbursement.
implementation_steps: Apply for financing well ahead of when funds are actually needed
Maintain a minimum cash reserve to cover essential costs during financing delays
Sequence project activities so financing-dependent items are not on the critical path where avoidable
Maintain an open line of communication with the lender on disbursement timelines

description: Reduce the risk of ZRA tax compliance penalties affecting project finances.
implementation_steps: Engage a local accountant or tax advisor familiar with SME obligations
Maintain accurate, up-to-date financial records throughout the project
Set calendar reminders for VAT, PAYE and other filing deadlines
Budget explicitly for applicable taxes and duties rather than treating them as an afterthought

description: Address insurance gaps that leave project assets such as hardware and equipment exposed.
implementation_steps: Review existing business insurance to identify coverage gaps for new project assets
Obtain appropriate cover for high-value equipment (e.g. servers, POS devices)
Keep an up-to-date asset register with values for insurance purposes
Review coverage annually or after major asset purchases

description: Manage the effect of local inflationary pressure on labour and contractor costs over the life of the project.
implementation_steps: Include an inflation adjustment clause in longer-term contractor agreements
Review labour cost assumptions at each major project phase
Build a modest annual cost escalation into multi-year budget forecasts
Track official inflation figures and factor them into re-forecasting

description: Address local ICT skills shortages through training and external partnerships.
implementation_steps: Partner with local universities or colleges for interns or graduate placements
Provide targeted training to existing staff on the new system
Document key procedures so non-specialist staff can perform basic tasks
Budget for short-term external support contracts for specialised tasks

description: Reduce the impact of staff turnover during implementation through knowledge retention practices.
implementation_steps: Maintain up-to-date documentation of system configuration and processes
Cross-train at least two people for each critical role
Conduct structured handover sessions when staff leave
Retain access logs and credentials centrally rather than with individuals

description: Avoid single points of failure arising from over-reliance on one subject-matter expert.
implementation_steps: Pair the expert with an understudy from the start of the project
Schedule regular knowledge-sharing sessions
Record key decisions and their rationale in a shared log
Plan work so the expert is not the sole approver for critical tasks

description: Strengthen project management capacity for complex implementations.
implementation_steps: Appoint a dedicated project coordinator, even on a part-time basis
Use a simple shared task tracker for visibility across the team
Hold short weekly status meetings with clear action items
Escalate resourcing gaps to management as early as possible

description: Reduce early productivity loss and errors through structured onboarding for new hires.
implementation_steps: Prepare a standard onboarding checklist covering systems, processes and contacts
Assign a buddy or mentor for the first few weeks
Provide access to documentation before the new hire's first day where possible
Check in formally at 30 and 90 days to catch early issues

description: Reduce the risk of staff burnout from unsustainable workloads during intensive project phases.
implementation_steps: Monitor working hours during crunch periods and flag sustained overtime
Rotate on-call or high-pressure duties across the team where possible
Build recovery time into the schedule after intensive delivery phases
Encourage open reporting of workload concerns without penalty

description: Address difficulty attracting skilled staff to project sites outside Lusaka or other major centres.
implementation_steps: Offer remote or hybrid arrangements for roles that do not require an on-site presence
Provide relocation or travel allowances where budget allows
Partner with regional colleges to source and train local talent
Consider phased or rotational staffing models for hard-to-fill locations

description: Close skills gaps between existing staff capabilities and the technical requirements of the new system.
implementation_steps: Conduct a skills assessment against system requirements before rollout
Provide targeted training for identified gaps rather than generic training
Pair less experienced staff with more skilled colleagues during early use
Reassess skills needs periodically as the system evolves

description: Reduce risk from inadequate succession planning for key leadership or decision-making roles.
implementation_steps: Identify critical roles with no clear successor
Provide stretch assignments to potential successors ahead of need
Document leadership decision-making processes, not just technical processes
Review succession plans at least annually

description: Reduce risk from relying on informal or undocumented staffing arrangements common in some SME settings.
implementation_steps: Formalise roles and responsibilities in writing, even for informal or family-run teams
Clarify decision-making authority to avoid ambiguity during the project
Document who has access to which systems and accounts
Review informal arrangements periodically as the business grows

description: Reduce ambiguity in requirements through structured requirements-gathering sessions.
implementation_steps: Hold dedicated workshops with key users to document requirements
Use simple templates such as user stories or process diagrams for non-technical review
Obtain written sign-off on requirements before development starts
Maintain a single requirements document as the source of truth

description: Manage frequent change requests through a lightweight change control process.
implementation_steps: Log all change requests in a shared register
Assess impact on time, cost and quality before approval
Batch minor changes into defined review cycles rather than ad hoc approval
Communicate approved and rejected changes to all stakeholders

description: Align technical design with business requirements through iterative validation with end users.
implementation_steps: Build a working prototype or mock-up early and review it with end users
Run short iterative development cycles rather than one long build phase
Validate each major module against documented requirements before proceeding
Involve end users directly in user-acceptance testing

description: Resolve conflicting departmental priorities through formal prioritisation.
implementation_steps: Establish a project steering committee with representation from affected departments
Use a simple impact-versus-effort scoring method to prioritise competing requests
Document and communicate agreed priorities to all parties
Revisit priorities only at fixed review points rather than continuously

description: Prevent gold-plating, where the team adds features beyond agreed requirements, consuming time and budget.
implementation_steps: Define and communicate a clear minimum viable feature set
Require justification and sign-off for any feature not in the original requirements
Track scope additions separately to make gold-plating visible
Review delivered features against the original requirements document at completion

description: Reduce disputes and rework arising from ambiguous acceptance criteria.
implementation_steps: Write specific, testable acceptance criteria for each requirement
Review acceptance criteria with both business and technical stakeholders
Reference acceptance criteria explicitly during user-acceptance testing
Avoid vague terms such as 'user-friendly' without a measurable definition

description: Manage scope risk arising from regulatory requirement changes part-way through the project.
implementation_steps: Assign responsibility for monitoring relevant regulatory developments
Design flexible, configurable system components where compliance requirements may change
Assess the impact of any regulatory change on scope, cost and schedule promptly
Engage a compliance advisor for significant regulatory shifts

description: Avoid delivery delays caused by a poorly defined minimum viable product (MVP).
implementation_steps: Explicitly define what is and is not included in the MVP before development starts
Prioritise MVP features using a simple must-have versus nice-to-have framework
Get stakeholder sign-off on the MVP definition specifically, not just the full requirements
Plan subsequent releases for features deferred from the MVP

description: Resolve stakeholder disagreement on what constitutes project success before it derails delivery.
implementation_steps: Facilitate a session to agree explicit, measurable success criteria early in the project
Document agreed success criteria and circulate for sign-off
Refer back to agreed criteria when disagreements arise later
Escalate unresolved disagreements to a designated decision-maker

description: Reduce risk from undocumented tribal knowledge driving requirements instead of documented processes.
implementation_steps: Interview long-serving staff to capture informal but critical process knowledge
Convert captured tribal knowledge into documented requirements or SOPs
Validate documented processes with the people who currently perform them
Store documentation centrally so it does not depend on any one individual

description: Mitigate operational disruption from load shedding through power continuity planning.
implementation_steps: Install UPS or inverter backup for essential equipment
Schedule power-dependent tasks around published load-shedding timetables
Consider solar backup for critical sites where budget allows
Maintain offline or manual procedures for short outages

description: Address unreliable connectivity at branch locations through redundancy and offline capability.
implementation_steps: Provision a secondary internet connection or mobile data backup at critical sites
Design the system to queue transactions offline and sync when connectivity returns
Test connectivity-dependent features under low-bandwidth conditions
Document manual fallback procedures for extended outages

description: Manage regulatory and compliance change risk through ongoing monitoring and flexible system design.
implementation_steps: Assign a team member to monitor relevant regulatory updates, such as data protection requirements
Design data-handling features to be configurable rather than hard-coded
Consult a local legal or compliance advisor before major data-related decisions
Maintain an audit trail of data access and changes

description: Reduce delays caused by dependency on external approvals from bodies such as banks or regulators.
implementation_steps: Identify all required external approvals at project start and map their typical timelines
Submit applications as early as possible with complete documentation
Maintain a single point of contact with each external party
Build realistic approval lead times explicitly into the project schedule

description: Manage exposure to broader currency and macroeconomic instability affecting business confidence and costs.
implementation_steps: Monitor key macroeconomic indicators relevant to the business (exchange rate, inflation, interest rates)
Diversify suppliers across currencies where practical to reduce single-currency exposure
Maintain flexible budgeting that can absorb moderate macroeconomic shifts
Review project viability if macroeconomic conditions shift significantly

description: Address community or local relations issues that can disrupt operations at a project site.
implementation_steps: Engage local community leaders and stakeholders early and transparently
Communicate project timelines and any disruptions to nearby residents or businesses
Establish a simple channel for community concerns to be raised and addressed
Document community engagement activities for accountability

description: Reduce losses from vandalism or theft of equipment at project or branch sites.
implementation_steps: Install basic physical security measures (locks, CCTV where affordable) at equipment sites
Maintain an up-to-date, serial-numbered asset register
Insure high-value equipment against theft and damage
Vet and monitor access for contractors and casual workers on site

description: Reduce risk from dependence on a single telecom provider for critical connectivity.
implementation_steps: Identify all points where the project depends on a single telecom provider
Provision a secondary SIM or provider as backup where feasible
Negotiate service-level agreements with the primary provider where possible
Monitor provider outage history when selecting or renewing contracts

description: Manage risk from political or policy uncertainty affecting the broader business climate.
implementation_steps: Monitor relevant policy developments through reputable local business news sources
Build flexibility into contracts and plans to accommodate policy shifts
Engage industry associations that track and advocate on policy issues
Avoid over-committing resources to activities highly sensitive to a single policy outcome

description: Reduce weather-related damage to infrastructure and equipment during Zambia's rainy season.
implementation_steps: Assess site drainage and flood risk before installing sensitive equipment
Use weatherproof enclosures for outdoor or semi-outdoor equipment
Schedule vulnerable installation work outside peak rainy months where possible
Include weather-related damage in insurance and contingency planning
